title: YOLO v1 to v3
concept: YOLO evolved from a single-grid design to a multi-scale system utilizing anchor boxes for improved small-object detection.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary advantage of YOLO's single-stage design compared to two-stage systems?

options.text: High processing speed, allowing real-time object detection
It is always 100% accurate on all datasets
It does not require labels for training

exercises.explain: Correct! Single-pass detection makes YOLO extremely fast and suitable for real-time video.

cards.title: Iterating to Greatness
cards.body: The original YOLO v1 was a revolution, but it had a major weakness: it struggled to detect small objects (like birds in the sky) and clumps of objects (like a flock of birds). How did researchers solve this?

cards.title: YOLO v1 to v3 Evolution
cards.body: YOLO evolved rapidly. YOLOv2 introduced anchor boxes (preset box shapes) and batch normalization. YOLOv3 added multi-scale predictions, which means the network predicts objects at three different grid sizes to detect both large and small objects.

cards.title: The Lens Zoom
cards.body: Imagine a camera with only one fixed lens (YOLOv1). It's hard to see things very close or very far. Upgrading to a camera with a zoom lens (YOLOv3) lets you capture a wide-angle view, a medium view, and a close-up view to find objects of all sizes.

cards.title: Multi-Scale Detection
cards.body: In a single street image, YOLOv3 uses a coarse 13x13 grid to find large trucks, a medium 26x26 grid to find cars, and a fine 52x52 grid to find tiny distant traffic cones.

cards.title: Multi-Scale Speed Cost
cards.body: You might think predicting at three scales makes YOLOv3 three times slower. In fact, by sharing the convolutional features in a unified backbone (Darknet-53), the speed cost was tiny, while small object detection doubled in accuracy.

cards.title: YOLOv1 vs. YOLOv3 Predictions
cards.body: How scale predictions changed in early YOLO versions:

left.text:
One S x S grid resolution
Struggles with small, clustered objects
No anchor boxes

right.text:
Three grid resolutions
Handles small, medium, and large objects
Uses preset anchor boxes

exercises.prompt: What key feature did YOLOv3 introduce to improve the detection of small objects?

options.text: Predicting bounding boxes at three different grid scales
Converting images to black and white before detection
Removing all convolutional layers from the network
Running the image backward through the network

exercises.explain: Correct! Predicting at three different scales allows YOLOv3 to capture small features at high resolutions.

exercises.prompt: True or False: YOLOv2 introduced anchor boxes, which are predefined shapes that help the model guess object dimensions.
exercises.explain: True. Anchor boxes give the model reference shapes (like tall/thin for people or wide/short for cars), making regression easier.

exercises.sentence: YOLOv2 and v3 use preset bounding box templates called ___ boxes to guide coordinate predictions.
exercises.explain: Anchor boxes act as templates for bounding box predictions.

exercises.prompt: What was the main weakness of the original YOLOv1?

options.text: It struggled to detect small objects or objects grouped close together
It was too slow to run on any computer
It could only output black and white bounding boxes

exercises.explain: Correct! YOLOv1 had rigid grid constraints, making it hard to detect multiple small objects close together.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a drone detection system for small birds. Why would you choose YOLOv3 over the original YOLOv1?

options.text: YOLOv3 uses multi-scale feature maps, allowing it to detect tiny objects on its high-resolution grid layers.
YOLOv1 cannot run on drones at all.
YOLOv3 does not require an anchor box, saving computation.
YOLOv1 is only designed to detect dogs.

exercises.explain: Exactly! YOLOv3's multi-scale prediction branches are specifically designed to catch small objects that get lost in coarse grids.
